summary: Jeff Atwood explores the bikeshed effect—the tendency for discussions to expand on simple topics while complex technical issues receive less debate—and connects it to procrastination psychology. He cites research showing that abstract thinking leads to procrastination while concrete, action-oriented thinking promotes execution. Drawing from his experience with Stack Overflow, he observes that soft, broad questions attract disproportionate attention compared to narrow technical ones. Atwood argues this dynamic is a form of procrastination that even skilled developers fall prey to, and advocates for quickly moving past abstract discussions to actually building things.
key_points: The bikeshed effect states that discussion time is inversely proportional to topic complexity—anyone can debate paint colors, but few can argue about reactor design
Abstract thinking promotes procrastination while concrete thinking about nuts and bolts encourages immediate action
Open source projects are especially vulnerable to attracting people more interested in discussion than writing code
Stack Overflow experienced this phenomenon with soft questions getting more engagement than technical coding questions
The value of a software feature is inversely proportional to the amount of discussion it generates
money_quotes: Consensus is hardest to achieve in technical questions that are simple to understand and easy to have an opinion about.
Who are we kidding? It's fun to discuss what color the bikeshed should be painted.
Those who thought about the questions abstractly were much more likely to procrastinate – and in fact some never got around to the assignment at all.
I find that the amount of discussion on a software feature is inversely proportional to its value.
The sooner you can get away from airy abstractions, and down to the nuts and bolts of building the damn thing, the better off you – and your project – will be.
What's the hardest thing you have to do every day? Deciding what to ignore, so you can stop procrastinating and get stuff done.
The next time you feel yourself getting drawn into a protracted bikeshed discussion, consider: shouldn't you be building something instead?
key_insight: Endless debates about simple topics aren't just inefficient communication—they're a disguised form of procrastination that keeps teams from building what actually matters.
